reasoning: Taylor Swift's "The Life of a Showgirl" debuted securely at #1 with a colossal 4 million units and, by November 2025, remains there for its fifth consecutive week. However, equivalent units have decayed predictably down to 120,000 per week. To hit 18 consecutive weeks, the album would need to remain at #1 relentlessly from its mid-October release all the way to roughly mid-February. Because Billboard ranks album streams and purchases cumulatively, achieving this feat requires maintaining high numbers not only across late November/Black Friday releases but critically straight through Christmas. During December, historical Billboard charts reveal that holiday classics (e.g., Michael Buble's "Christmas" or Bing Crosby) consistently surge past 100k-150k units, regularly returning to #1 on the Billboard 200. Furthermore, major hip-hop or pop surprise drops (like Kendrick Lamar or Drake) frequently eclipse the 70-80k mark that a decaying album generates in its third month. No modern album successfully defends 18 consecutive weeks across the Christmas surge unless it is a Christmas album itself or a freak generational occurrence paired with virtually no meaningful competition. Thus, a mid-October non-holiday release faces an unpassable December hurdle. The probability fundamentally remains at the forecast minimum of 2%.
